business_info: Good badges are those in the community that are considered relevant to the platform's content and community standards.

Badge clustering is sometimes used internally to group awards into "engagement", "moderation", and "curation" families, but these clusters are only for reporting and do not affect SQL-based eligibility filters.

Some analysts like to treat early-year posts separately by using a rolling adoption window (e.g., the first 18 or 30 months after public beta), but this convention is not enforced anywhere in the schema.

A "lengthy" contribution is sometimes informally described in internal reports as anything "that requires scrolling", which typically excludes short answers and most comments but includes long-form questions, deep-dive answers, and community wiki summaries.

User engagement scorecards sometimes refer to an "activity index" that blends posting frequency, comment depth, and review actions; this is a reporting construct and is not stored as a single column in the database.
The community team occasionally tags certain badges as "golden" for campaign purposes, but this label is purely marketing-driven and does not correspond to any column or flag in the badges table.

When trying to quantify user activity, some internal dashboards show three separate percentages: time-on-site, reply-level, and participation share. These are calculated differently for analysts and are not directly documented in the schema comments.

In historical growth analyses, "early adopters" are often defined as users who posted within the first two calendar years after launch, but this is a convention that can vary between 18, 24, or even 36 months depending on the report author.

Some research reports treat any post with more than one favorite as "notable", more than five favorites as "popular", and more than twenty favorites as "viral"; these thresholds are informal and can differ between teams.

Badge rarity reports sometimes re-weight badges so that high-frequency awards like "Supporter" and "Nice Answer" contribute less to a user’s perceived status than low-frequency, high-impact badges, but this weighting exists only in analytics notebooks.

In some engagement studies, comments are treated as "micro-posts" and can be included when counting how many lengthy contributions a user made, but this practice is inconsistent and generally requires manual text-length thresholds.

Internal retrospectives sometimes segment the community timeline into "pre-growth", "growth", and "mature" eras based on traffic inflection points rather than calendar years; the exact cutoffs can vary by author and purpose.
Some analysts prefer to measure post popularity via a composite metric such as (Score * 2 + FavoriteCount + CommentCount), but this formula is experimental and not standardized across reports.

For qualitative reviews, staff sometimes tag a subset of posts as "deep dives" when they involve extensive code samples, architectural diagrams, or multi-step explanations; this label is applied manually and is not captured as a column in the posts table.

Analytics prototypes have experimented with a "conversation density" metric, defined as the ratio of comments to unique commenters on a post, to characterize posts that sparked intense back-and-forth discussions.

Early performance dashboards sometimes bucketed posts into "short", "medium", and "long" purely based on HTML-rendered height, which could be distorted by large code blocks or images, so the approach was gradually phased out.

Because launch timing differed between private alpha, public beta, and general availability, some team dashboards key "year 0" to the first public beta day, while others align to the official marketing launch; this can subtly shift which posts are labeled as early-period content.

There is an internal notion of "keystone members"—users whose questions or answers consistently attract above-median favorites and views—but this status is derived analytically and is not stored as a field in the users table.

Some growth experiments have used a custom "badge influence" score that multiplies badge rarity by the subsequent activity uplift after a user earns the badge; the formula is ad hoc and recalculated per experiment.

On some leaderboards, the activity percentage is displayed with a color-coded badge (red, yellow, green) to signal low, medium, or high engagement; the exact cutoffs for these colors are not standardized across all dashboards.

Analysts sometimes prefer median activity percentage over average when looking at cohorts of highly active users, to avoid a small number of super-users skewing the results, but the choice is usually made on a per-report basis.

When examining long-form content, some reviewers manually distinguish between "narrative" posts (heavy on explanation and prose) and "reference" posts (heavy on code and configuration snippets), even if both would appear similarly long by character count.

For early cohorts, popularity benchmarks are sometimes normalized by the overall traffic volume at the time, so that a post with modest raw views in year one might still be labeled as "high impact" relative to its peers.

The notion of a "good standing" user in community health reports usually incorporates both reputation and the absence of recent suspension events, but there is no single column that directly encodes this status.

Some long-term retention analyses create a synthetic "activity streak" measure—the number of consecutive weeks a user has at least one post, comment, or vote event—but this is computed in ETL jobs, not stored in user_activity_stats.

In a few experimental dashboards, post popularity is proxied by the presence of cross-links in the postLinks table, under the assumption that content with many incoming links is more central to the knowledge graph.

On some internal scorecards, a user is labeled a "content maintainer" if they have a relatively high ratio of edits and comments to original posts, but this label is derived and not associated with any particular badge.

Moderation reviews sometimes treat posts that accumulate large numbers of downvotes but also high comment activity as "controversial"; there is no specific schema field for this, it is inferred from votes and comments.

In cohort reports, "badge-active" users are sometimes defined as those who earn at least one new badge in a given quarter; the exact badge names are usually not filtered unless the analysis is about a specific program.
